concepts.title: Understanding the principles in Fundamental Principle of Counting
concepts.conceptBody:
Fundamental principle of counting

There are 2 principles which form the basis of fundamental principle of counting.

If one operation / activity can be performed in $\text{m}$ ways and a second activity can be performed in $\text{n}$ ways then.

  1. the number of ways of performing both the operations will be $m\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }n$, this is also known as principle of multiplication.
  2. the number of ways of performing one of the two activities will be $m+n$. This is also known as principle of addition.

Q) You have cleared IIT and you are going to a garment shop for purchasing some clothes for yourself, which you will take to IIT. You select 5 jeans and 10 shirts from which you can buy. In how many ways can you purchase 

  1. only 1 shirt and 1 jean? 
  2. only one of shirt or jeans? 

Solution: 

Though it is sad that you have to buy only one of shirts & jeans.

Let’s solve the question now

(i) You have to buy a pair of shirt & jeans. If you take one jean then you have 10 options for shirt.

Same is the case for other jeans.

i.e. every time you select a jean, there are 10 options for a shirt.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Total ways $=5\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }10=50$ (i.e. fundamental principle of multiplication)

(ii) Now you have to select only one of either shirt or jeans.

You can select 1 jean out of 5 in 5 ways & 1 shirt out of 10 in 10 ways. for selecting only one of them, you can have

(no. of ways of selecting one shirt) + (no. of ways of selecting one jeans)

$\Rightarrow$ Total ways $=10+5=15$ (i.e. fundamental principal of addition)

Q) Find the total number of ways of answering 5 objective type questions, each having 4 choices. 

Solution: 

There are 5 questions,

And each question has 4 options i.e. each question can be answered in 4 ways

$\therefore$ Total no. of ways = $\begin{array}{lllll}4 & 4 & 4 & 4 & 4\end{array}$

$=4^5$

Note: We will solve the questions as if we are filling spaces.

Q) Five persons entered a lift cabin of a 8 floor building. Suppose each of them can leave the cabin independently at any floor beginning with first. Find the total no. of ways in which each of five persons can leave the cabin. 

(i) at any of the 7 floors 

(ii) at different floors 

Solution: 

Suppose there are 5 persons ${{\text{A}}_{1}}{{\text{A}}_{2}}{{\text{A}}_{3}}{{\text{A}}_{4}}{{\text{A}}_{5}}$

$\overline{{{\text{A}}_{1}}}\overline{{{\text{A}}_{2}}}\overline{{{\text{A}}_{3}}}\overline{{{\text{A}}_{4}}}\overline{{{\text{A}}_{5}}}$

(i) for this part, any person can leave any floor i.e. all persons have 7 options to choose from.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \frac{7}{{{\text{A}}_{1}}} & \frac{7}{{{\text{A}}_{2}}}\frac{7}{{{\text{A}}_{3}}}\frac{7}{{{\text{A}}_{4}}}\frac{7}{{{\text{A}}_{5}}} \\ \end{array}$

$\therefore $ Total ways $=\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 7 & 7 & 7 & 7 & 7={{7}^{5}} \\ \end{array}$

(ii) Now all persons have to leave at different floors. Suppose we start with ${{\text{A}}_{1}}$, he drops at floor 2. Now ${{\text{A}}_{2}}$ has $\left( 7-1 \right)=6$ options as he cannot leave at floor 2 according to the question.

$\begin{array}{l} \text{ So }{{\text{A}}_{3}}\text{ now }\text{ will }\left( 7-2 \right)\text{ options }\And\text{ so, }\text{ on }\frac{7}{{{\text{A}}_{1}}}\frac{6}{{{\text{A}}_{2}}}\frac{5}{{{\text{A}}_{3}}}\frac{4}{{{\text{A}}_{4}}}\frac{3}{{{\text{A}}_{5}}} \\ \therefore \text{ total }\text{ ways }=\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 7 & 6 & 5 & 4 & 3 \\ \end{array} \\ =2520 \\ \end{array}$

Q) In how many ways can six persons be arranged in a row? 

Solution: 

Arranging a given set of $\text{n}$ different objects is equivalent to filling $\text{n}$ places.

Now suppose ${{A}_{1}},{{A}_{2}},{{A}_{3}},{{A}_{4}},{{A}_{5}},{{A}_{6}}$ to be the six persons which are to be arranged in a row.

For 6 people there are 6 positions to be filled and at one position only one person can stand.

So, if we start from ${{A}_{1}}$, he has 6 positions to choose from. For ${{A}_{2}}$, he has 5 as 1 position has already been occupied by ${{A}_{1}}\And $ so on for ${{A}_{3}}{{A}_{4}}{{A}_{5}}\And {{A}_{6}}$

$\frac{6}{\mathrm{~A}_1} \quad \frac{5}{\mathrm{~A}_2} \quad \frac{4}{\mathrm{~A}_3} \quad \frac{3}{\mathrm{~A}_4} \quad \frac{2}{\mathrm{~A}_5} \quad \frac{1}{\mathrm{~A}_6}$

$\therefore$ Total ways = 6 5 4 3 3 2 1

$=6 !$

Note: We can start from any person $\left( {{\text{A}}_{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{A}}_{3}}\ldots {{\text{A}}_{6}} \right)$, in this example we started from ${{\text{A}}_{1}}$ but the answer will be same for each case as it is just a representation.

Q) How many 3-digit numbers can be formed with using digits $0,2,3,4,5$ and 6, (a) without repetition (b) with repetition 

Solution: 

3-digit can be represented by 3 places

for both the cases, 0 cannot be at hundreds place. (Obvious)

(a) With repetition 

for this case, we can use the digits only once per number with 0 not at hundreds place.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Total numbers = 5 5 4

$=100$

(b) With repetition 

$\left\{ \begin{matrix} \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ The }\!\!~\!\!\text{ term }\!\!~\!\!\text{ used }\!\!~\!\!\text{ in }\!\!~\!\!\text{ previous }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ place }\!\!~\!\!\text{ can }\!\!~\!\!\text{ also }\!\!~\!\!\text{ be }\!\!~\!\!\text{ used }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \end{matrix} \right\}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Total numbers = 5 6 6

$=180$

Note: Unless stated in the question itself that repetition is allowed we do not take repetition.

Q) You have to invite 5 of your friends to your birthday party. In how many ways can you send invitation cards to them, if you have 3 servants to carry the cards? 

Solution: 

You can send the card to any of your friend through any of your 3 servants.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Each friend can receive invitation by 3 ways, i.e., from 3 servants

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \frac{3}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{F}}_{1}}} & \frac{3}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{F}}_{2}}} & \frac{3}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{F}}_{3}}} & \frac{3}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{F}}_{4}}} & \frac{3}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{F}}_{5}}} \\ \end{array}$

Total ways $=\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 3 & 3 & 3 & 3 & 3 \\ \end{array}$

$={{3}^{5}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ ways }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

Q) You have 5 digits 4, 5, 6, 7 & 8 

(i) find the number of 4 digit even numbers which can be formed by using above digits. 

(ii) numbers which are divisible by 5 and are greater than 6000. 

Solution: 

(i) This part requires to find out even numbers. We know even numbers end with 0, 2, 4, 6 $\And 8$ so our last digit has to be either one of them and from the digits given to us $(4,5,6$, $7,8)4,6\And 8$ are the possible options.

any digit from the 5 possible digits.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Total numbers $=\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 5 & 5 & 5 & 3 \\ \end{array}$

$=375$

(ii) For a digit to be a multiple of 5, its ones digit should be either 0 or 5. From the digits given to us only 5 is the option. Also, the should be greater than 6000, so in thousands digit only 3 options are there i.e. 6, 7 & 8.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$Total numbers $\begin{array}{llll}=3 & 5 & 5 & 1\end{array}$

= 75

concepts.title: Permutations
concepts.conceptBody:
Permutations

Each of the arrangement which can be made by taking some or all things is called a permutation. Let us understand this with the help of an example.

Suppose, India, Australia & Pakistan are playing a triseries tournament. In how ways the result can come out.

So, there are 6 arrangements possible (though out of which we guys will prefer the first two.)

Note: You can see the order plays an important role in arrangement.

Now, write the number of permutations of letters A, B, C, D taking 3 at a time.

Let us see how many cases can we make I will start with A

6 with A $\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B }\!\!~\!\!\text{ C }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B }\!\!~\!\!\text{ D }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A }\!\!~\!\!\text{ C }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A }\!\!~\!\!\text{ C }\!\!~\!\!\text{ D }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A }\!\!~\!\!\text{ D }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A }\!\!~\!\!\text{ D }\!\!~\!\!\text{ C }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \end{array}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }6 \right.$ with B $\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A }\!\!~\!\!\text{ C }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A }\!\!~\!\!\text{ D }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B }\!\!~\!\!\text{ C }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B }\!\!~\!\!\text{ D }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B }\!\!~\!\!\text{ C }\!\!~\!\!\text{ D }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B }\!\!~\!\!\text{ D }\!\!~\!\!\text{ C }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \end{array}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }6 \right.$ with C $\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ C }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ C }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A }\!\!~\!\!\text{ D }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ C }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ C }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B }\!\!~\!\!\text{ D }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ C }\!\!~\!\!\text{ D }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ C }\!\!~\!\!\text{ D }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \end{array}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }6 \right.$ with D $\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ D }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ D }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A }\!\!~\!\!\text{ C }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ D }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ D }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B }\!\!~\!\!\text{ C }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ D }\!\!~\!\!\text{ C }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ D }\!\!~\!\!\text{ C }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \end{array} \right.$

So, in all there are 24 permutations possible.

Notation: 

The number of permutations of $n$ distinct things taken $r$ at a time is given by ${{~}^{n}}{{P}_{r}}$ or $P\left( n,r \right)$ and its value is

${{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\mathbf{P}}_{\text{r}}}=\frac{\text{n}!}{\left( \text{n}-\text{r} \right)!}$

So, if we apply this to the previous case we just covered, 4 things taking 3 at a time

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{~}^{4}}{{\text{P}}_{3}}=\frac{4!}{1!}=24$ arrangements

Logic: ${{~}^{n}}{{\text{P}}_{\text{r}}}$ is equivalent to saying, the numbers of ways in which we can fill up $\text{r}$ places, when we have $n$ different things to choose from.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \underset{\text{r }\!\!~\!\!\text{ places }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}{\mathop{\underset{}{\mathop{\frac{\text{n}}{1}\frac{\left( \text{n}-1 \right)}{2}\frac{\left( \text{n}-2 \right)}{3}\frac{\left( \text{n}-3 \right)}{4}\ldots \ldots \frac{\left( \text{n}-\text{r}+1 \right)}{\text{r}}}}\,}}\, \\ {} & {{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{P}}_{\text{r}}}=\frac{\text{n}!}{\left( \text{n}-\text{r} \right)!}=\text{n}\left( \text{n}-1 \right)\left( \text{n}-2 \right)\ldots \left( \text{n}-\left( \text{r}-1 \right) \right) \\ \end{array}$

Here notice that we have have considered the case when no repetition is allowed. {because then only $2,3,4,\ldots $ places are filled by $\left( \text{n}-1 \right),\left( \text{n}-2 \right)\ldots $ values $\}$

Now,

Permutations when repetition is allowed 

It simple to understand. Here again there are $r$ places but

$\underset{\text{r }\!\!~\!\!\text{ places }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}{\mathop{\underset{}{\mathop{\frac{\text{n}}{1}\frac{\text{n}}{2}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\text{n}}{3}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\text{n}}{4}\ldots \frac{\text{n}}{\text{r}}}}\,}}\,$

now every place can take any of the $n$ values, hence total permutations $={{n}^{r}}$

Some properties of ${{~}^{n}}{{\text{P}}_{\text{r}}}$

  1. $\text{P}\left( \text{n},\text{n} \right)=\text{P}\left( \text{n},\text{n}-1 \right)$
  2. $P\left( n,r \right)=n\cdot P\left( n-1,r-1 \right)$

concepts.title: Number of Permutations/Arrangements when all the objects are not unique(or distinct)
concepts.conceptBody:
Permutations / Arrangements when all objects are not distinct 

Formula: In a total of $\text{n}$ objects if there are $\text{p}$, of one kind, ${{\text{p}}_{2}}$ are alike of second kind & so on, such that ${{\text{p}}_{1}}+{{\text{p}}_{2}}+\ldots {{\text{p}}_{\text{n}}}=\text{n}$, the total arrangements possible

$=\left( \frac{\text{n}!}{{{\text{p}}_{1}}!{{\text{p}}_{2}}!{{\text{p}}_{3}}!\ldots {{\text{p}}_{\text{n}}}!} \right)$

Note: If two p are alike of one kind & q are alike to second kind & all others are distinct, then the formula boils down to simply

$=\frac{\text{n}!}{\text{p}!\text{q}!}\left\{ \text{ as }\text{ others }\text{ which }\text{ are }\text{ different }\text{ will }\text{ be }\text{ adding }1!\text{ only } \right\}$

Q) How many words can be formed using the letter A thrice, letter B twice & letter C thrice? 

Solution: 

In this question all objects are not distinct

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{ A }\text{ is }\text{ repeated } & 3\text{ times } \\ \text{ B } & 2\text{ times } \\ \text{ C } & 3\text{ times } \\ {} & \text{ in }\text{ all }3+2+3=8\text{ letter }\text{ word } \\ \therefore & \text{ Total }\text{ words }=\frac{8!}{3!2!3!}=560 \\ \end{array}$

Q) How many arrangements can be made with the letters of the word 'MATHEMATICS'? In how many of them vowels are together? 

Solution: 

There are 11 letters in the word 'MATHEMATICS' of which two are M's, two are T's, two are A's and all other are distinct. So, Required number of arrangements $=\frac{11!}{2!\times 2!\times 2!}=4989600$

There are 4 vowels viz. A, E, A, I. Considering these four vowels as one letter we have 8 letters (M, T, H, M, T, C, S and one letter obtained by combining all vowels), out of which $\text{M}$ occurs twice, $\text{T}$ occurs twice and the rest all different. These 8 letters can be arranged in $\frac{8!}{2!\times 2!}$ ways.

But the four vowels (A, E, A, I) can be put together in $\frac{4!}{2!}$ ways.

Hence, the total number of arrangements in which vowels are always together

$=\frac{8!}{2!\times 2!}\times \frac{4!}{2!}=120960$

Q) If all the letters of the word 'AGAIN' be arranged as in a dictionary, what is the fiftieth word? 

Solution: 

This is the common dictionary problem. Let us sec how to solve it.

  1. In dictionary the words at each stage are arranged in alphabetical order. Starting with the letter A, and arranging the other four letters GAIN, we obtain 4! = 24 words.
  2. Thus, there are 24 words which start with A. These are the first 24 words.

Then, starting with G, and arranging the other four letters A, A, I, N in different ways, we obtain $\frac{4!}{2!}=\frac{24}{2}=12$ words.

Thus, there are 12 words, which start with G. So, until now we have covered $24+12=36$ first words.

  1. Now, we start with I. The remaining 4 letters A, G, A, N can be arranged in $\frac{4!}{2!}=12$ ways. So, there are 12 words, which start with I.

Thus, we have so far constructed 48 words.

  1. The 49th word will start from $\text{N}$. Then the next alphabet will be A, then A, then $\text{G}\And $ then I, hence the word is NAAGI and hence the 50th word is NAAIG.

Q) The letters of the word 'RANDOM' are written in all possible orders and these words are written out as in a dictionary. Find the rank of the word 'RANDOM'. 

Solution: 

In a dictionary the words at each stage are arranged in alphabetical order. In the given problem we must therefore consider the words beginning with $\text{A},\text{D},\text{M},\text{N},\text{O},\text{R}$ in order. A will occur in the first place as often as there are ways of arranging the remaining 5 letters all at a time i.e. A will occur 5! times. Similarly, D, M, N, O will occur in the first place the same number of times.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Number of words starting with $\text{A}=5!=120$

Number of words starting with $\text{D}=5!=120$

Number of words starting with $\text{M}=5!=120$

Number of words starting with $\text{N}=5!=120$

Number of words starting with $\text{O}=5!=120$

Number of words beginning with $\text{R}$ is 5! but one of these words is the word RANDOM. So, we first find the number of words beginning with RAD and RAM.

No. of words starting with $\text{RAD}=3!=6$

No. of words starting with $\text{RAM}=3!=6$

$\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{ with }\text{ the }\text{ remaining } \\ 3\text{ alphabets } \\ \end{array} \right\}$

Now, the words beginning with 'RAN'. One of these words is the word RANDOM itself.

The first word beginning with RAN is the word RANDMO and the next word is RANDOM.

$\therefore$ Rank of RANDOM $=5120+26+2=614$

Q) How many four-letter words can be formed using the word 'INEFFECTIVE'? 

Solution: 

There is a total of 11 letters in the word INEFFECTIVE

$\begin{array}{l} 3-\text{{E}' }\text{ s} \\ 2-\text{{F}' }\text{ s} \\ 2-\text{{I}' }\text{ s} \\ 1-\text{C},\text{T},\text{N},\text{V} \\ \end{array}$

The 4-letter word can be made in the following manner

Case $1:3$ alike letter $\And 1$ different

Case 2 : 2 alike letters of one kind & 2 letters alike of other kind.

Case 3 : 2 alike letters & 2 different

Case 4 : all different.

Note: No case like 1 alike, 3 diff. As it makes no sense.

∴ Total number of 4 letter words

$=24+18+540+840$

= 1422

The total number of permutations of the letters of the word "BANANA" is
